excerpts:
Title: MCP Best Practices: Architecture & Implementation Guide > 🏗️ Architectural Design Principles > 2. Defense in Depth Security Model
Content:
**Layer security controls throughout your architecture.**
```
# Example: Multi-layer security implementation class SecureMCPServer : def __init__ ( self ): # Layer 1: Network isolation self . bind_address = "127.0.0.1" # Local only # Layer 2: Authentication self . auth_handler = JWTAuthHandler () # Layer 3: Authorization self . permissions = CapabilityBasedACL () # Layer 4: Input validation self . validator = StrictSchemaValidator () # Layer 5: Output sanitization self . sanitizer = DataSanitizer () @authenticate @authorize ([ "read_files" ]) @validate_input @sanitize_output def read_file ( self , path : str ) -> str : # Business logic here pass
```
**Security Layers** :
**Network** : Firewall rules, VPN access, local binding
**Authentication** : Strong identity verification
**Authorization** : Granular permission controls
**Validation** : Input sanitization and schema enforcement
**Monitoring** : Comprehensive audit logging and aler
citations.title: MCP Best Practices: Architecture & Implementation Guide – Model Context Protocol （MCP）

excerpts: This overview of the Model Context Protocol (MCP) discusses its scope and core concepts, and provides an example demonstrating each core concept.Read more
citations.title: Architecture overview

excerpts: We introduce a structured engineering lifecycle encompassing workflow decomposition, multi-agent design patterns, Model Context Protocol(MCP), and tool integration, deterministic orchestration, Responsible-AI considerations, and environment-aware deployment strategies.
Modern agentic workflows integrate LLMs with external tools, structured memory, search functions, databases, Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers, cloud services, and API-driven environments

es such as ways to decompose workflows into agents, making choices between tool calls and MCP actions [ mcp1 , mcc ] , ways to design deterministic orchestration, and methods to avoid implicit behaviors that lead to LLM drift or unpredictable execution paths

operational challenges include running workflows reliably in production environments; managing concurrency, failures, retries, logging, and cost efficiency; securing tool access; monitoring agent traces; and ensuring reproducibility across model updates.
We complement these principles with nine actionable best practices aimed at improving determinism, reliability, observability, and maintainability.

excerpts:
Section Title: AI Agent Security: Best Practices Guide 2025 > OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications (2026)
Content:
| Risk ID | Risk Name | Description | Severity |
| ASI01 | Agent Goal Hijack | Attackers redirect agent objectives via manipulated instructions, tool outputs, or external content | Critical |
| ASI02 | Tool Misuse & Exploitation | Agents misuse legitimate tools due to prompt injection, misalignment, or unsafe delegation | Critical |
| ASI03 | Identity & Privilege Abuse | Attackers exploit inherited credentials, delegated permissions, or agent-to-agent trust | Critical |
| ASI04 | Supply Chain Vulnerabilities | Malicious or tampered tools, descriptors, models, or agent personas compromise execution | High |
| ASI05 | Unexpected Code Execution | Agents generate or execute attacker-controlled code without proper validation | Critical |
| ASI06 | Memory & Context Poisoning | Persistent corruption of agent memory, RAG stores, or contextual knowledge | High |
| ASI07 | Insecure Inter-Agent Communication | Attackers spoof or intercept messages exchanged between agents without verification | High |
| ASI08 | Cascading Failures | Single agent error propagates through interconnected agents, amplifying damage | Medium |
| ASI09 | Human-Agent Trust Exploitation | Agents generate polished, confident explanations that mislead human operators | Medium |
| ASI10 | Rogue Agents | Compromised AI agents exhibiting misalignment or self-directed unauthorized actions | High |

Section Title: AI Agent Security: Best Practices Guide 2025 > Understanding the Threat Landscape > 2. Memory & Context Poisoning (ASI06)
Content:
Unlike traditional AI models that forget after each session, agentic systems persist context across interactions—making memory both a strength and a critical vulnerability. Attackers can corrupt an agent's short-term or long-term memory with malicious or misleading data, causing the agent to behave incorrectly long after the initial attack. This is fundamentally different from prompt injection: the corruption is persistent.
citations.title: AI Agent Security: Best Practices Guide 2025

excerpts: An example of “chain-of-failure” can be, an attacker doesn't just 'Poison Memory.' They use **Memory Poisoning (ASI06)** to perform an **Agent Goal Hijack (ASI01)** . Because the agent has **Excessive Agency (ASI03)** , it uses its high-level permissions to trigger **Unexpected Code Execution (ASI05)** via the Code Interpreter tool.

Another highly relevant excerpt explicitly mentions cascading failures, stating that a single agent error can propagate through interconnected agents, amplifying damage. This aligns closely with the described risk of multi-agent propagation leading to widespread system malfunction. A third excerpt discusses memory poisoning as a persistent vulnerability that persists across interactions, which supports the broader context of how multi-agent systems can experience lasting or propagating effects when memory/state is compromised, contributing to broader system instability. Taken together, these excerpts substantiate the propagation and cascading risk described in the finegrained field value by illustrating both cross-agent propagation and persistent effects within agent ecosystems.

excerpts: An example of “chain-of-failure” can be, an attacker doesn't just 'Poison Memory.' They use **Memory Poisoning (ASI06)** to perform an **Agent Goal Hijack (ASI01)** . Because the agent has **Excessive Agency (ASI03)** , it uses its high-level permissions to trigger **Unexpected Code Execution (ASI05)** via the Code Interpreter tool.
citations.title: Architecting Trust: A NIST-Based Security Governance Framework for AI Agents | Microsoft Community Hub

excerpts:
Section Title: AI Agent Security: Best Practices Guide 2025 > OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications (2026)
Content:
| Risk ID | Risk Name | Description | Severity |
| ASI01 | Agent Goal Hijack | Attackers redirect agent objectives via manipulated instructions, tool outputs, or external content | Critical |
| ASI02 | Tool Misuse & Exploitation | Agents misuse legitimate tools due to prompt injection, misalignment, or unsafe delegation | Critical |
| ASI03 | Identity & Privilege Abuse | Attackers exploit inherited credentials, delegated permissions, or agent-to-agent trust | Critical |
| ASI04 | Supply Chain Vulnerabilities | Malicious or tampered tools, descriptors, models, or agent personas compromise execution | High |
| ASI05 | Unexpected Code Execution | Agents generate or execute attacker-controlled code without proper validation | Critical |
| ASI06 | Memory & Context Poisoning | Persistent corruption of agent memory, RAG stores, or contextual knowledge | High |
| ASI07 | Insecure Inter-Agent Communication | Attackers spoof or intercept messages exchanged between agents without verification | High |
| ASI08 | Cascading Failures | Single agent error propagates through interconnected agents, amplifying damage | Medium |
| ASI09 | Human-Agent Trust Exploitation | Agents generate polished, confident explanations that mislead human operators | Medium |
| ASI10 | Rogue Agents | Compromised AI agents exhibiting misalignment or self-directed unauthorized actions | High |

Section Title: AI Agent Security: Best Practices Guide 2025 > Understanding the Threat Landscape > 2. Memory & Context Poisoning (ASI06)
Content:
Unlike traditional AI models that forget after each session, agentic systems persist context across interactions—making memory both a strength and a critical vulnerability. Attackers can corrupt an agent's short-term or long-term memory with malicious or misleading data, causing the agent to behave incorrectly long after the initial attack. This is fundamentally different from prompt injection: the corruption is persistent.
citations.title: AI Agent Security: Best Practices Guide 2025

Collectively, these excerpts substantiate the stated field value by detailing the components (spans, metrics, tool execution, conversation tracking, token usage) and the purpose (portable, end-to-end observability across vendors/frameworks) of GenAI semantic conventions in OpenTelemetry.

excerpts:
**Status:**
This span represents a client call to Generative AI model or service that generates a response or requests a tool call based on the input prompt.
**Span name** SHOULD be `{gen_ai.operation.name} {gen_ai.request.model}` .
Semantic conventions for individual GenAI systems and frameworks MAY specify different span name format
and MUST follow the overall [guidelines for span names](https://github.com/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-specification/blob/v1.37.0/specification/trace/api.md) .
**Span kind** SHOULD be `CLIENT` and MAY be set to `INTERNAL` on spans representing
call to models running in the same process. It’s RECOMMENDED to use `CLIENT` kind
when the GenAI system being instrumented usually runs in a different process than its
client or when the GenAI call happens over instrumented protocol such as HTTP.
**Span status** SHOULD follow the Recording Errors document.
**Attributes:**
 ...

**Status:**
Describes tool execution span.
`gen_ai.operation.name` SHOULD be `execute_tool` .
**Span name** SHOULD be `execute_tool {gen_ai.tool.name}` .
GenAI instrumentations that can instrument tool execution calls SHOULD do so,
unless another instrumentation can reliably cover all supported tool types.
MCP tool executions may also be traced by the corresponding MCP instrumentation .
Tools are often executed directly by application code. Application developers
are encouraged to follow this semantic convention for tools invoked by their
own code and to manually instrument any tool calls that automatic
instrumentations do not cover.
**Span kind** SHOULD be `INTERNAL` .
**Span status** SHOULD follow the Recording Errors document.
**Attributes:**
 ...

**[4] `gen_ai.conversation.id` :** Instrumentations SHOULD populate conversation id when they have it readily available
for a given operation, for example:
when client framework being instrumented manages conversation history
(see [LlamaIndex chat store](https://docs.llamaindex.ai/en/stable/module_guides/storing/chat_stores/) )
when instrumenting GenAI client libraries that maintain conversation on the backend side
(see [AWS Bedrock agent sessions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/agents-session-state.html) , [OpenAI Assistant threads](https://platform.openai.com/docs/api-reference/threads) )
Application developers that manage conversation history MAY add conversation id to GenAI and other
spans or logs using custom span or log record processors or hooks provided by instrumentation
libraries.

**[1] `gen_ai.operation.name` :** If one of the predefined values applies, but specific system uses a different name it’s RECOMMENDED to document it in the semantic conventions for specific GenAI system and use system-specific name in the instrumentation. If a different name is not documented, instrumentation libraries SHOULD use applicable predefined value.
**[2] `gen_ai.provider.name` :** The attribute SHOULD be set based on the instrumentation’s best
knowledge and may differ from the actual model provider.
Multiple providers, including Azure OpenAI, Gemini, and AI hosting platforms
are accessible using the OpenAI REST API and corresponding client libraries,
but may proxy or host models from different providers.
The `gen_ai.request.model` , `gen_ai.response.model` , and `server.address` attributes may help identify the actual system in use.
The `gen_ai.provider.name` attribute acts as a discriminator that
identifies the GenAI telemetry format flavor specific to that provider
within GenAI semantic conventions.
It SHOULD be set consistently with provider-specific attributes and signals.
For example, GenAI spans, metrics, and events related to AWS Bedrock
should have the `gen_ai.provider.name` set to `aws.bedrock` and include
applicable `aws.bedrock.*` attributes and are not expected to include `openai.*` attributes.

excerpts:
Semantic conventions for Generative AI operations are defined for the following signals:
Events : Semantic Conventions for Generative AI inputs and outputs - *events* .
Metrics : Semantic Conventions for Generative AI operations - *metrics* .
Model spans : Semantic Conventions for Generative AI model operations - *spans* .
Agent spans : Semantic Conventions for Generative AI agent operations - *spans* .

Technology specific semantic conventions are defined for the following GenAI system:
Anthropic : Semantic Conventions for Anthropic.
Azure AI Inference : Semantic Conventions for Azure AI Inference.
AWS Bedrock : Semantic Conventions for AWS Bedrock.
OpenAI : Semantic Conventions for OpenAI.

excerpts: We're thrilled to announce that the OpenLLMetry semantic conventions have officially become part of OpenTelemetry! This milestone is a significant step in standardizing the observability of large language model (LLM) based applications.

By integrating our semantic conventions with OpenTelemetry, you'll benefit from:
**Enhanced Observability** : Gain deeper insights into your LLM applications with standardized metrics, traces, and logs.
An official OpenTelemetry workgroup was founded to identify and define a standardized vocabulary and specification for the observability of LLM-based applications.
citations.title: New GenAI Semantic Conventions in OpenTelemetry

Excerpts mentioning tool scope, governance, and risk management further corroborate the security-by-design framework in which OWASP top-10 categories would operate, even if they do not repeat the exact OWASP label. The remaining excerpts discuss the broader governance framework, testing approaches, and accountability, which provide contextual evidence about enforcing and operationalizing risk controls that would align with applying an OWASP Top 10 for LLMs, but do not introduce new explicit OWASP labels. excerpts: **Govern:** Establish a culture of "Security by Design." Define who is responsible for an agent's actions. *Crucial for agents: Who is liable if an agent makes an unauthorized API call?*
| **Tool Scoping:** Is the agent's access limited only to the specific APIs it needs? | ASI02: Tool Misuse |
**Manage:** Deploying guardrails and monitoring. This is where you prioritize risks like "Excessive Agency" (OWASP LLM08).
**Measure:** How do you test for "agentic" risks? Conduct **Red Teaming** for agents and assess **Groundedness** scores.

In this post, Umesh Nagdev and Abhi Singh, showcase a **Security Governance Framework** for LLM Agents (used interchangeably as Agents in this article). We aren't just checking boxes; we are mapping the [**NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 100-1)**](https://www.nist.gov/itl/ai-risk-management-framework) directly onto the [**Microsoft Foundry**](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/products/ai-foundry/) ecosystem.

An example of “chain-of-failure” can be, an attacker doesn't just 'Poison Memory.' They use **Memory Poisoning (ASI06)** to perform an **Agent Goal Hijack (ASI01)** . Because the agent has **Excessive Agency (ASI03)** , it uses its high-level permissions to trigger **Unexpected Code Execution (ASI05)** via the Code Interpreter tool.
**Map:** Identify the "surface area" of the agent. This includes the LLM, the system prompt, the tools (APIs) it can access, and the data it retrieves (RAG).

t:
The framework attempts to guide "Governance" defines accountability and intent, whereas "Map, Measure, Manage" define enforcement.

**[1] `gen_ai.operation.name` :** If one of the predefined values applies, but specific system uses a different name it’s RECOMMENDED to document it in the semantic conventions for specific GenAI system and use system-specific name in the instrumentation. If a different name is not documented, instrumentation libraries SHOULD use applicable predefined value.
**[2] `gen_ai.provider.name` :** The attribute SHOULD be set based on the instrumentation’s best
knowledge and may differ from the actual model provider.
Multiple providers, including Azure OpenAI, Gemini, and AI hosting platforms
are accessible using the OpenAI REST API and corresponding client libraries,
but may proxy or host models from different providers.
The `gen_ai.request.model` , `gen_ai.response.model` , and `server.address` attributes may help identify the actual system in use.
The `gen_ai.provider.name` attribute acts as a discriminator that
identifies the GenAI telemetry format flavor specific to that provider
within GenAI semantic conventions.
It SHOULD be set consistently with provider-specific attributes and signals.
For example, GenAI spans, metrics, and events related to AWS Bedrock
should have the `gen_ai.provider.name` set to `aws.bedrock` and include
applicable `aws.bedrock.*` attributes and are not expected to include `openai.*` attributes.

excerpts: **Continuous monitoring:** Tracking agent actions, decisions, and interactions in real time to surface anomalies, unexpected behaviors, or performance drift.

**Tracing:** Capturing detailed execution flows, including how agents reason through tasks, select tools, and collaborate with other agents or services. This helps answer not just “what happened,” but “why and how did it happen?”
**Logging:** Records agent decisions, tool calls, and internal state changes to support debugging and behavior analysis in agentic AI workflows.
**Evaluation:** Systematically assessing agent outputs for quality, safety, compliance, and alignment with user intent—using both automated and human-in-the-loop methods.

Section Title: AI Agent Security: Best Practices Guide 2025 > Essential Security Controls for AI Agents > Comprehensive Audit Logging
Content:
Maintain detailed logs of all AI agent activities aligned with OpenTelemetry's AI Agent Observability standards for security monitoring and incident response:
**Prompt Logging:** Record all prompts sent to AI agents (sanitizing sensitive data first), including token usage and tool interactions.
**Response Tracking:** Log AI-generated responses and agent decision paths for forensic analysis.
**File Modification Tracking:** Audit all files created, modified, or deleted by AI agents.
**SIEM Integration:** Integrate AI telemetry with platforms like Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, or Sumo Logic for enterprise-wide visibility.

```
# Example: Multi-layer security implementation class SecureMCPServer : def __init__ ( self ): # Layer 1: Network isolation self . bind_address = "127.0.0.1" # Local only # Layer 2: Authentication self . auth_handler = JWTAuthHandler () # Layer 3: Authorization self . permissions = CapabilityBasedACL () # Layer 4: Input validation self . validator = StrictSchemaValidator () # Layer 5: Output sanitization self . sanitizer = DataSanitizer () @authenticate @authorize ([ "read_files" ]) @validate_input @sanitize_output def read_file ( self , path : str ) -> str : # Business logic here pass
```

excerpts: **Manage:** Deploying guardrails and monitoring. This is where you prioritize risks like "Excessive Agency" (OWASP LLM08).

An example of “chain-of-failure” can be, an attacker doesn't just 'Poison Memory.' They use **Memory Poisoning (ASI06)** to perform an **Agent Goal Hijack (ASI01)** . Because the agent has **Excessive Agency (ASI03)** , it uses its high-level permissions to trigger **Unexpected Code Execution (ASI05)** via the Code Interpreter tool.

In this post, Umesh Nagdev and Abhi Singh, showcase a **Security Governance Framework** for LLM Agents (used interchangeably as Agents in this article). We aren't just checking boxes; we are mapping the [**NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 100-1)**](https://www.nist.gov/itl/ai-risk-management-framework) directly onto the [**Microsoft Foundry**](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/products/ai-foundry/) ecosystem.

t:
The framework attempts to guide "Governance" defines accountability and intent, whereas "Map, Measure, Manage" define enforcement.
**Govern:** Establish a culture of "Security by Design." Define who is responsible for an agent's actions. *Crucial for agents: Who is liable if an agent makes an unauthorized API call?*
**Map:** Identify the "surface area" of the agent. This includes the LLM, the system prompt, the tools (APIs) it can access, and the data it retrieves (RAG).
**Measure:** How do you test for "agentic" risks? Conduct **Red Teaming** for agents and assess **Groundedness** scores.
| **Tool Scoping:** Is the agent's access limited only to the specific APIs it needs? | ASI02: Tool Misuse |

excerpts:
Semantic conventions for Generative AI operations are defined for the following signals:
Events : Semantic Conventions for Generative AI inputs and outputs - *events* .

citations.title: Semantic conventions for generative AI systems | OpenTelemetry

excerpts:
**Status:**
This span represents a client call to Generative AI model or service that generates a response or requests a tool call based on the input prompt.
**Span name** SHOULD be `{gen_ai.operation.name} {gen_ai.request.model}` .
Semantic conventions for individual GenAI systems and frameworks MAY specify different span name format
and MUST follow the overall [guidelines for span names](https://github.com/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-specification/blob/v1.37.0/specification/trace/api.md) .
**Span kind** SHOULD be `CLIENT` and MAY be set to `INTERNAL` on spans representing
call to models running in the same process. It’s RECOMMENDED to use `CLIENT` kind
when the GenAI system being instrumented usually runs in a different process than its
client or when the GenAI call happens over instrumented protocol such as HTTP.
**Span status** SHOULD follow the Recording Errors document.
**Attributes:**
 ...

**[1] `gen_ai.operation.name` :** If one of the predefined values applies, but specific system uses a different name it’s RECOMMENDED to document it in the semantic conventions for specific GenAI system and use system-specific name in the instrumentation. If a different name is not documented, instrumentation libraries SHOULD use applicable predefined value.
**[2] `gen_ai.provider.name` :** The attribute SHOULD be set based on the instrumentation’s best
knowledge and may differ from the actual model provider.
Multiple providers, including Azure OpenAI, Gemini, and AI hosting platforms
are accessible using the OpenAI REST API and corresponding client libraries,
but may proxy or host models from different providers.
The `gen_ai.request.model` , `gen_ai.response.model` , and `server.address` attributes may help identify the actual system in use.
The `gen_ai.provider.name` attribute acts as a discriminator that
identifies the GenAI telemetry format flavor specific to that provider
within GenAI semantic conventions.
It SHOULD be set consistently with provider-specific attributes and signals.
For example, GenAI spans, metrics, and events related to AWS Bedrock
should have the `gen_ai.provider.name` set to `aws.bedrock` and include
applicable `aws.bedrock.*` attributes and are not expected to include `openai.*` attributes.

**Status:**
Describes tool execution span.
`gen_ai.operation.name` SHOULD be `execute_tool` .
**Span name** SHOULD be `execute_tool {gen_ai.tool.name}` .
GenAI instrumentations that can instrument tool execution calls SHOULD do so,
unless another instrumentation can reliably cover all supported tool types.
MCP tool executions may also be traced by the corresponding MCP instrumentation .
Tools are often executed directly by application code. Application developers
are encouraged to follow this semantic convention for tools invoked by their
own code and to manually instrument any tool calls that automatic
instrumentations do not cover.
**Span kind** SHOULD be `INTERNAL` .
**Span status** SHOULD follow the Recording Errors document.
**Attributes:**
 ...

**[4] `gen_ai.conversation.id` :** Instrumentations SHOULD populate conversation id when they have it readily available
for a given operation, for example:
when client framework being instrumented manages conversation history
(see [LlamaIndex chat store](https://docs.llamaindex.ai/en/stable/module_guides/storing/chat_stores/) )
when instrumenting GenAI client libraries that maintain conversation on the backend side
(see [AWS Bedrock agent sessions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/agents-session-state.html) , [OpenAI Assistant threads](https://platform.openai.com/docs/api-reference/threads) )
Application developers that manage conversation history MAY add conversation id to GenAI and other
spans or logs using custom span or log record processors or hooks provided by instrumentation
libraries.

excerpts: We're thrilled to announce that the OpenLLMetry semantic conventions have officially become part of OpenTelemetry! This milestone is a significant step in standardizing the observability of large language model (LLM) based applications.

By integrating our semantic conventions with OpenTelemetry, you'll benefit from:
**Enhanced Observability** : Gain deeper insights into your LLM applications with standardized metrics, traces, and logs.
An official OpenTelemetry workgroup was founded to identify and define a standardized vocabulary and specification for the observability of LLM-based applications.

In this post, Umesh Nagdev and Abhi Singh, showcase a **Security Governance Framework** for LLM Agents (used interchangeably as Agents in this article). We aren't just checking boxes; we are mapping the [**NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 100-1)**](https://www.nist.gov/itl/ai-risk-management-framework) directly onto the [**Microsoft Foundry**](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/products/ai-foundry/) ecosystem.
**Govern:** Establish a culture of "Security by Design." Define who is responsible for an agent's actions. *Crucial for agents: Who is liable if an agent makes an unauthorized API call?*
| **Tool Scoping:** Is the agent's access limited only to the specific APIs it needs? | ASI02: Tool Misuse |

An example of “chain-of-failure” can be, an attacker doesn't just 'Poison Memory.' They use **Memory Poisoning (ASI06)** to perform an **Agent Goal Hijack (ASI01)** . Because the agent has **Excessive Agency (ASI03)** , it uses its high-level permissions to trigger **Unexpected Code Execution (ASI05)** via the Code Interpreter tool.
**Manage:** Deploying guardrails and monitoring. This is where you prioritize risks like "Excessive Agency" (OWASP LLM08).
**Map:** Identify the "surface area" of the agent. This includes the LLM, the system prompt, the tools (APIs) it can access, and the data it retrieves (RAG).

excerpts:
Title: MCP Best Practices: Architecture & Implementation Guide > 🏗️ Architectural Design Principles > 2. Defense in Depth Security Model
Content:
**Layer security controls throughout your architecture.**
```
# Example: Multi-layer security implementation class SecureMCPServer : def __init__ ( self ): # Layer 1: Network isolation self . bind_address = "127.0.0.1" # Local only # Layer 2: Authentication self . auth_handler = JWTAuthHandler () # Layer 3: Authorization self . permissions = CapabilityBasedACL () # Layer 4: Input validation self . validator = StrictSchemaValidator () # Layer 5: Output sanitization self . sanitizer = DataSanitizer () @authenticate @authorize ([ "read_files" ]) @validate_input @sanitize_output def read_file ( self , path : str ) -> str : # Business logic here pass
```
**Security Layers** :
**Network** : Firewall rules, VPN access, local binding
**Authentication** : Strong identity verification
**Authorization** : Granular permission controls
**Validation** : Input sanitization and schema enforcement
**Monitoring** : Comprehensive audit logging and aler

Section Title: AI Agent Security: Best Practices Guide 2025 > Essential Security Controls for AI Agents > Comprehensive Audit Logging
Content:
Maintain detailed logs of all AI agent activities aligned with OpenTelemetry's AI Agent Observability standards for security monitoring and incident response:
**Prompt Logging:** Record all prompts sent to AI agents (sanitizing sensitive data first), including token usage and tool interactions.
**Response Tracking:** Log AI-generated responses and agent decision paths for forensic analysis.
**File Modification Tracking:** Audit all files created, modified, or deleted by AI agents.
**SIEM Integration:** Integrate AI telemetry with platforms like Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, or Sumo Logic for enterprise-wide visibility.

Section Title: AI Agent Security: Best Practices Guide 2025 > Essential Security Controls for AI Agents > Comprehensive Audit Logging
Content:
Maintain detailed logs of all AI agent activities aligned with OpenTelemetry's AI Agent Observability standards for security monitoring and incident response:
citations.title: AI Agent Security: Best Practices Guide 2025

excerpts: **Evaluation:** Systematically assessing agent outputs for quality, safety, compliance, and alignment with user intent—using both automated and human-in-the-loop methods.

**Tracing:** Capturing detailed execution flows, including how agents reason through tasks, select tools, and collaborate with other agents or services. This helps answer not just “what happened,” but “why and how did it happen?”
**Logging:** Records agent decisions, tool calls, and internal state changes to support debugging and behavior analysis in agentic AI workflows.
**Continuous monitoring:** Tracking agent actions, decisions, and interactions in real time to surface anomalies, unexpected behaviors, or performance drift.

Together, these excerpts support the idea that a governance framework emphasizing observability and auditing would leverage standardized instrumentation, including trace spans and metrics, to monitor and audit agent orchestration activities. Specific connections include: the definition of signals for GenAI operations (events, metrics, model spans, agent spans) that provide observable telemetry; client call spans that document interactions with GenAI models or services; recommended span naming and provider-discriminating attributes that aid auditing and attribution; and sections dedicated to GenAI metrics that quantify observable behavior in client applications. These pieces collectively underpin the capability to observe, log, and audit the behavior of an agent orchestrator service through standardized observability instrumentation.

excerpts:
Semantic conventions for Generative AI operations are defined for the following signals:
Events : Semantic Conventions for Generative AI inputs and outputs - *events* .
Metrics : Semantic Conventions for Generative AI operations - *metrics* .
Model spans : Semantic Conventions for Generative AI model operations - *spans* .
Agent spans : Semantic Conventions for Generative AI agent operations - *spans* .
citations.title: Semantic conventions for generative AI systems | OpenTelemetry

excerpts:
**[1] `gen_ai.operation.name` :** If one of the predefined values applies, but specific system uses a different name it’s RECOMMENDED to document it in the semantic conventions for specific GenAI system and use system-specific name in the instrumentation. If a different name is not documented, instrumentation libraries SHOULD use applicable predefined value.
**[2] `gen_ai.provider.name` :** The attribute SHOULD be set based on the instrumentation’s best
knowledge and may differ from the actual model provider.
Multiple providers, including Azure OpenAI, Gemini, and AI hosting platforms
are accessible using the OpenAI REST API and corresponding client libraries,
but may proxy or host models from different providers.
The `gen_ai.request.model` , `gen_ai.response.model` , and `server.address` attributes may help identify the actual system in use.
The `gen_ai.provider.name` attribute acts as a discriminator that
identifies the GenAI telemetry format flavor specific to that provider
within GenAI semantic conventions.
It SHOULD be set consistently with provider-specific attributes and signals.
For example, GenAI spans, metrics, and events related to AWS Bedrock
should have the `gen_ai.provider.name` set to `aws.bedrock` and include
applicable `aws.bedrock.*` attributes and are not expected to include `openai.*` attributes.
**[4] `gen_ai.conversation.id` :** Instrumentations SHOULD populate conversation id when they have it readily available
for a given operation, for example:
when client framework being instrumented manages conversation history
(see [LlamaIndex chat store](https://docs.llamaindex.ai/en/stable/module_guides/storing/chat_stores/) )
when instrumenting GenAI client libraries that maintain conversation on the backend side
(see [AWS Bedrock agent sessions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/agents-session-state.html) , [OpenAI Assistant threads](https://platform.openai.com/docs/api-reference/threads) )
Application developers that manage conversation history MAY add conversation id to GenAI and other
spans or logs using custom span or log record processors or hooks provided by instrumentation
libraries.
**Status:**
Describes tool execution span.
`gen_ai.operation.name` SHOULD be `execute_tool` .
**Span name** SHOULD be `execute_tool {gen_ai.tool.name}` .
GenAI instrumentations that can instrument tool execution calls SHOULD do so,
unless another instrumentation can reliably cover all supported tool types.
MCP tool executions may also be traced by the corresponding MCP instrumentation .
Tools are often executed directly by application code. Application developers
are encouraged to follow this semantic convention for tools invoked by their
own code and to manually instrument any tool calls that automatic
instrumentations do not cover.
**Span kind** SHOULD be `INTERNAL` .
**Span status** SHOULD follow the Recording Errors document.
**Attributes:**
 ...

**Status:**
This span represents a client call to Generative AI model or service that generates a response or requests a tool call based on the input prompt.
**Span name** SHOULD be `{gen_ai.operation.name} {gen_ai.request.model}` .
Semantic conventions for individual GenAI systems and frameworks MAY specify different span name format
and MUST follow the overall [guidelines for span names](https://github.com/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-specification/blob/v1.37.0/specification/trace/api.md) .
**Span kind** SHOULD be `CLIENT` and MAY be set to `INTERNAL` on spans representing
call to models running in the same process. It’s RECOMMENDED to use `CLIENT` kind
when the GenAI system being instrumented usually runs in a different process than its
client or when the GenAI call happens over instrumented protocol such as HTTP.
**Span status** SHOULD follow the Recording Errors document.
**Attributes:**
 ...

are encouraged to follow this semantic convention for tools invoked by their
own code and to manually instrument any tool calls that automatic
instrumentations do not cover.

By integrating our semantic conventions with OpenTelemetry, you'll benefit from:
**Enhanced Observability** : Gain deeper insights into your LLM applications with standardized metrics, traces, and logs.
An official OpenTelemetry workgroup was founded to identify and define a standardized vocabulary and specification for the observability of LLM-based applications.

**Measure:** How do you test for "agentic" risks? Conduct **Red Teaming** for agents and assess **Groundedness** scores.
**Manage:** Deploying guardrails and monitoring. This is where you prioritize risks like "Excessive Agency" (OWASP LLM08).
**Govern:** Establish a culture of "Security by Design." Define who is responsible for an agent's actions. *Crucial for agents: Who is liable if an agent makes an unauthorized API call?*
**Map:** Identify the "surface area" of the agent. This includes the LLM, the system prompt, the tools (APIs) it can access, and the data it retrieves (RAG).

In this post, Umesh Nagdev and Abhi Singh, showcase a **Security Governance Framework** for LLM Agents (used interchangeably as Agents in this article). We aren't just checking boxes; we are mapping the [**NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 100-1)**](https://www.nist.gov/itl/ai-risk-management-framework) directly onto the [**Microsoft Foundry**](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/products/ai-foundry/) ecosystem.

t:
The framework attempts to guide "Governance" defines accountability and intent, whereas "Map, Measure, Manage" define enforcement.

executive_summary.description: A high-level overview of the most critical best practices for designing, building, and operating a secure, reliable, and observable agent orchestrator service.

core_principles.description: The fundamental guiding principles for agentic system design, such as starting with the simplest effective architecture, maintaining transparency, and separating deterministic workflow from ambiguous agentic reasoning.

pattern_name.description: The name of the orchestration pattern (e.g., Direct model call, Multi-agent orchestration).

description.description: A detailed description of how the pattern works.

when_to_use.description: Guidance on the types of problems or tasks for which this pattern is most suitable.

considerations.description: Key factors to consider when implementing this pattern, such as complexity, cost, and potential failure modes.

framework_name.description: The name of the security or risk management framework (e.g., NIST AI RMF, MITRE ATLAS).

focus_area.description: The primary focus of the framework, such as risk governance, vulnerability taxonomy, or attack techniques.

best_for.description: The target audience or team that would benefit most from this framework (e.g., Executives, Security Teams, Red Teams).

description.description: A brief description of the framework's purpose and strengths.

security_governance_frameworks.description: An overview of key security and risk management frameworks applicable to AI agents, including NIST AI RMF (Govern, Map, Measure, Manage), MITRE ATLAS for adversarial tactics, Google SAIF, and the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications.

risk_id.description: The official identifier for the risk from the OWASP Agentic Security Initiative (e.g., ASI01).

risk_name.description: The name of the agent-specific security risk (e.g., Agent Goal Hijack, Memory & Context Poisoning).

description.description: A detailed explanation of the security risk and how it manifests in agentic systems.

severity.description: The assessed severity level of the risk (e.g., Critical, High, Medium).

owasp_top_10_for_agentic_apps.description: A detailed list and explanation of the OWASP Top 10 risks specifically for autonomous agentic applications (ASI), including Agent Goal Hijack (ASI01), Tool Misuse (ASI02), Memory & Context Poisoning (ASI06), and Rogue Agents (ASI10).

critical_security_threats_and_mitigation.description: In-depth analysis of specific security threats like prompt injection, insecure tool usage, data leakage, memory poisoning, and supply chain vulnerabilities, along with actionable mitigation strategies for each.

identity_and_access_management.description: Best practices for managing agent identity and permissions, including the principle of least privilege, scoping tool access, using dedicated credentials, network isolation (VNet), and requiring human-in-the-loop (HITL) approvals for sensitive actions.

agent_observability_pillars.description: Explanation of the five pillars of agent observability: traditional metrics, logs, and traces, plus the agent-specific additions of evaluations and governance, which are essential for understanding non-deterministic systems.

opentelemetry_for_ai_standards.description: A detailed guide to the OpenTelemetry (OTel) semantic conventions for Generative AI. Covers standardized attributes for spans (e.g., agent, model, tool calls), metrics (e.g., token usage, duration), and events to ensure consistent telemetry.

platform_name.description: The name of the observability platform or tool (e.g., LangSmith, Arize, Azure AI Foundry).

capabilities.description: The key features and capabilities of the platform, such as tracing, evaluation, monitoring, or red teaming.

best_for.description: The primary use case or team the platform is best suited for (e.g., AI ops teams, security testing, enterprise validation).

observability_platforms_and_tools.description: A list of tools and platforms used for agent observability, including LangSmith, LangFuse, Arize Phoenix, and Azure AI Foundry. Details their capabilities in tracing, evaluation, and monitoring.

state_and_memory_management.description: Strategies for managing agent state and context, including techniques for handling limited context windows (compaction, summarization), persisting state for long-running tasks using durable stores, and using checkpointing for fault tolerance (e.g., in LangGraph).

reliability_and_error_handling.description: Patterns for building robust and reliable agentic systems, such as implementing timeouts, retries with exponential backoff, circuit breakers, graceful degradation with fallback models, and validating agent outputs before propagation.

performance_and_cost_optimization.description: Techniques for optimizing the performance and cost of agent orchestrators, including assigning smaller, cheaper models to simpler tasks, monitoring token consumption, implementing caching strategies, and using batching or streaming where appropriate.

strategy_name.description: The name of the testing or evaluation method (e.g., Adversarial Testing/Red Teaming, Groundedness Scoring).

description.description: A description of what the strategy entails and why it is important for agentic systems.

example_tool.description: An example tool or platform that can be used to implement this strategy (e.g., Azure AI Red Teaming Agent).

testing_and_evaluation_strategies.description: A comprehensive overview of methods for testing and evaluating agents, such as offline evaluation on 'golden datasets', LLM-as-judge scoring, A/B testing, groundedness scoring for RAG, and adversarial testing through AI red teaming.

name.description: The name of the framework or interface (e.g., LangGraph, Model Context Protocol).

type.description: The type of the entry, such as 'Framework', 'Protocol', or 'Platform'.

description.description: A brief overview of the framework or interface and its purpose in agent orchestration.

key_orchestration_frameworks_and_interfa.description: An overview of prominent frameworks and protocols for building agentic systems, including LangChain/LangGraph, Microsoft Azure AI Foundry, OpenAI Assistants, and the Model Context Protocol (MCP) for standardized tool interaction.

common_pitfalls_and_anti_patterns.description: A summary of common mistakes to avoid when implementing agent orchestration, such as creating unnecessary complexity, overlooking latency impacts, sharing mutable state between concurrent agents, and ignoring resource constraints.
